detail: **Framework:** Cloudflare Workers (edge runtime, fetch-based, no Node.js). **Stripe SDK:** v14.x (v15 has ESM incompatibility with Workers bundler). **HMAC Signing:** Web Crypto API (Workers-native, constant-time verification). **SMS:** Twilio REST API with Basic Auth. **Email:** Resend API. **Storage:** Cloudflare KV (eventually consistent, schema-on-read). **Cron:** Cloudflare Triggers (*/2 cron = every 2 minutes). **Rate Limiting:** Client-side timestamp check with tier-based intervals (free=30min, premium=2min). **Security:** HMAC-signed unsubscribe tokens, Stripe webhook signature verification, constant-time comparison for auth.

Test framework: Vitest v1.6 for both extension unit tests and backend integration tests — consistent tooling across workstreams, native ES module support (required by the extension's MV3 service worker), and first-class support via @cloudflare/vitest-pool-workers for Cloudflare Worker testing with miniflare KV mocks. No Jest (CommonJS-first, poor ES module ergonomics). Extraction strategy: pure functions only into src/slotFilters.js — background.js retains all chrome.* API calls and alarm/notification orchestration. This keeps the extracted module testable in Node.js without a Chrome extension harness. Known gap: the Stripe webhook route (/api/webhook) described in the original task does not exist in the current worker.js — integration tests are scoped to the four routes that actually exist; a placeholder test file should be committed to reserve the slot and fail loudly until the route is implemented.

Fix manifest permissions, add tabs permission, and build 3-step onboarding flow

No new dependencies introduced — both tasks use only React 19 useState/useEffect, native fetch API, Next.js 15 Route Handlers, and Tailwind v4 utility classes. The critical architectural decision is isolating client interactivity into dedicated components (PricingSection.tsx, EmailCaptureForm.tsx) so that page.tsx remains a Server Component — this preserves SSR/SEO benefits for the bulk of the page. The FAQ uses zero-JS <details>/<summary> with a CSS-only chevron animation via globals.css (not Tailwind plugin) because Tailwind v4's group-open: variant requires explicit CSS for the transform in some configurations. The /api/checkout route stub is added proactively to prevent 404s before Stripe integration is wired up. metadataBase in layout.tsx is non-negotiable for Next.js 15 OG image resolution — this is a common gotcha engineers miss.

All extension code is pure vanilla JS/CSS — no bundler, no npm, no CDN. MV3 CSP prohibits inline scripts and eval(); all event listeners use addEventListener() and all JS lives in separate files. Error backoff state (failureCount, lastError, lastErrorTime) lives in chrome.storage.local alongside config — no IndexedDB needed at this scale. Tier enforcement is dual-layered: alarm periodInMinutes is clamped at the alarm-creation level (background.js setupAlarm) AND checked at call time in checkAllLocations — belt-and-suspenders against alarm drift. host_permissions for api.nexus-alert.com must be declared in manifest.json for fetch() calls from popup context (not just service worker). The onboarding flow uses window.close() on finish rather than chrome.tabs.remove(tabId) to avoid needing the tabs permission during onboarding itself.

Stripe v14 pinned (not v15) due to ESM/fetch compatibility in Cloudflare Workers runtime. Web Crypto API used throughout for HMAC instead of Node crypto (Workers have no Node built-ins by default — avoid `nodejs_compat` flag unless necessary as it adds bundle overhead). Twilio integrated via raw fetch + Basic Auth rather than the Twilio SDK (SDK is Node-centric and adds ~500KB to bundle). Subscriber and license data kept as separate KV keys (`subscriber:<email>` vs `license:<email>`) to allow independent updates from Stripe webhooks without race conditions on the subscriber record. Free-tier rate limiting is enforced at the worker level via `last_checked_at` timestamp rather than a separate queue, keeping infrastructure minimal. All secrets via `wrangler secret put` (never in wrangler.toml or source control).

The core extension is production-ready. The critical path to paying customers is: (1) wire up Stripe + premium tier enforcement in the backend so revenue is possible, (2) polish the extension with onboarding and error handling so users actually retain, (3) complete the landing page conversion funnel with pricing and email capture, and (4) add tests + docs so the product can be maintained and deployed reliably.
